examples.title: Daemon Creation - Basic Implementation
examples.description: Complete C program demonstrating daemon creation with all standard steps
examples.explanation: This C program demonstrates the complete daemonization process: fork and parent exit, setsid to detach from terminal, optional second fork for security, chdir to root directory, umask setting, closing standard file descriptors, and redirecting to /dev/null. The daemon then writes to a log file and runs an infinite loop.

examples.title: Signal Handling in Daemons
examples.description: Pseudocode showing proper signal handling for daemon processes
examples.code:
DAEMON SIGNAL HANDLER
=======================

VARIABLES:
 running = TRUE
 reload_config = FALSE

FUNCTION setup_signal_handlers():
 // Install SIGHUP handler for config reload
 signal(SIGHUP, handle_sighup)
 
 // Install SIGTERM for graceful shutdown
 signal(SIGTERM, handle_sigterm)
 
 // Install SIGCHLD to reap children
 signal(SIGCHLD, handle_sigchld)
 
 // Ignore SIGHUP during reload to prevent race
 // Will be unblocked after reload completes

FUNCTION handle_sighup(signal_number):
 reload_config = TRUE
 log("SIGHUP received, reloading configuration...")
 reload_configuration()
 reload_config = FALSE

FUNCTION handle_sigterm(signal_number):
 log("SIGTERM received, shutting down gracefully...")
 cleanup_resources()
 close_log_file()
 running = FALSE

FUNCTION handle_sigchld(signal_number):
 WHILE (child_pid = waitpid(-1, NULL, WNOHANG)) > 0:
 log("Child process %d terminated", child_pid)
 // Prevents zombie processes

FUNCTION daemon_main_loop():
 setup_signal_handlers()
 
 WHILE running == TRUE:
 process_requests()
 sleep(1)
 
 EXIT 0
examples.explanation: Demonstrates proper signal handling in daemon processes: SIGHUP for configuration reload without restart, SIGTERM for graceful shutdown, and SIGCHLD handling to prevent zombie processes. This is essential for production daemons.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) per request + O(1) signal handling

examples.title: Systemd Service Unit Example
examples.description: Example systemd unit file for a daemon service
examples.code:
[Unit]
Description=My Custom Daemon Service
Documentation=man:myd(8)
After=network.target
Wants=network.target

[Service]
Type=simple
ExecStart=/usr/bin/myd --config /etc/myd.conf
ExecReload=/bin/kill -HUP $MAINPID
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5
User=mysql
Group=mysql
StandardOutput=journal
StandardError=journal

# Environment settings
Environment="MYD_HOME=/var/lib/myd"

# Resource limits
LimitNOFILE=65536
LimitNPROC=4096

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

# Commands:
# systemctl start myd.service
# systemctl stop myd.service
# systemctl reload myd.service
# systemctl status myd.service
# journalctl -u myd.service
examples.explanation: Shows how modern Linux systems use systemd to manage daemons. The unit file defines service properties: type (simple/oneshot/forking), startup command, restart policies, user/group to run as, logging to journal, and dependency relationships with other services.

examples.title: inetd/xinetd Configuration Pattern
examples.description: Demonstrates how transient daemons are spawned by inetd super-server
examples.code:
INETD CONFIGURATION (inetd.conf)
================================

FORMAT:
<service_name> <socket_type> <protocol> <wait> <user> <path> <args>

EXAMPLE ENTRIES:
ftp stream tcp nowait root /usr/sbin/ftpd ftpd -l
telnet stream tcp nowait root /usr/sbin/telnetd telnetd
finger stream tcp nowait nobody /usr/sbin/fingerd fingerd -w

PARAMETERS:
- service_name: port/service name from /etc/services
- socket_type: stream (TCP) or dgram (UDP)
- protocol: tcp, udp, or tcp6/udp6
- wait: nowait (spawn new process) or wait (single process)
- user: username to run daemon as
- path: absolute path to daemon executable
- args: command line arguments

WORKFLOW:
1. inetd listens on configured ports
2. Connection request arrives on port
3. inetd forks and execs the specified daemon
4. Daemon handles the request
5. Daemon exits after completing request
6. inetd continues listening

ADVANTAGE: Resources saved - service runs only when needed
DISADVANTAGE: Startup overhead for each new connection
examples.explanation: Shows how inetd operates as a meta-daemon that spawns other services on demand. This is the traditional approach for services that aren't constantly busy, reducing memory usage by not running all services continuously.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) listen + O(s) service + O(1) exit
